methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series C → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.7x) to $200M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $700M - $2.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: KoBold Metals
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $2B+

private.name: Watershed
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1.8B

private.name: Electric Hydrogen
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $1B+

private.name: Amogy
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series C), post-money $700M

data.narrative:
Fortera, a Series C-stage climate and energy company founded by Ryan Gilliam, has raised over $200M to date. The valuation analysis draws on a comparable set of 9 private companies and 1 public peer (LNZA) operating in the same sector, with particular focus on four same-stage climate tech businesses: KoBold Metals, Watershed, Electric Hydrogen, and Amogy.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, using the $200M total raised as a proxy baseline and applying a 1.7x step-up multiplier typical of Series C advancement, yields $340M. The peer-median approach references valuations across the four identified comparables, which range from $700M (Amogy) to $2.0B (KoBold Metals), producing a median of $1.4B. This wider dispersion reflects the heterogeneity of business models and maturity within the frontier climate sector at Series C stage.

The triangulated analysis produces a valuation range of $289M to $1.6B, with a median of $870M. The substantial gap between methods—driven primarily by the peer-median's higher positioning relative to the stage-progression result—reflects underlying uncertainty about Fortera's competitive positioning within its cohort. Key sensitivities include stage and capital efficiency relative to named peers, as well as the comparability of underlying technology risk and revenue traction. The wide range suggests additional diligence on unit economics and market-capture assumptions is warranted before final valuation setting.
